story:
My baby sister Sally’s crying wakes me up. She’s five; I’m ten. We share a room because our house has only two bedrooms. It’s a whimpering cry—the kind that means she didn’t get her way, not the kind that means she’s hurt—but I still hate hearing it. I reach through the dark, find her curly blonde hair, and stroke her head like I do my dog, Scruffy. “Hey, little’n, what’s wrong?” I whisper. That breaks the dam. She sobs and stammers that the Hat Man was standing at the foot of her bed, red eyes glowing, like folks say, and she just knows he came to kill her and eat her.

Up here in the mountains, everybody knows the Hat Man story: he shows up at night in a floppy felt hat, paralyzes you with his burning eyes, drags you off, and you never come back. Of course, at my age, I’m too old to believe in such things. Still, I hush her—“You’ll wake Daddy, and he ain’t gettin’ enough sleep as it is”—but the bedroom door opens anyway.

Daddy stands there in his boxers, small but broad in the shoulders, Army-short haircut, coal-mine tired. He sits on her bed and asks, soft as can be, “What’s the matter, honey?” Sally squeals about the Hat Man. Daddy’s jaw tightens. “Who told you about that mess?” “Bo,” she sniffles—our cousin. Daddy mutters he don’t know who to whip first, his halfwit nephew for telling her or his brother and sister-in-law for letting him believe it. Sally begs him not to whip nobody; last time he did, the cops hauled him to jail after some fellas made a pass at Mom at the county fair. Daddy turns red, not mad, just ashamed. “Don’t worry. Figure of speech,” he says.

Then he thinks and says, “Tell you what. You can sleep in your big brother’s bed tonight. You’ll be safe there. That alright, Jeb?” I nod. Truth is, I worry what the boys at school would say if they found out—especially Joey Bivens, who flunked fourth but never flunks a chance to run his mouth—but Sally matters more. She crawls into my bed, snuggles close, and soon she’s breathing steady. I fall asleep after.

In the morning, after breakfast, I get bored and take Scruffy into the woods across the road. He’s my best friend, Daddy’s “half beagle, half Jack Russell, and 100% mutt.” We head toward the big oak where Daddy carved a heart with Mom’s initials when he was twelve. Near the tree, Scruffy growls, then bolts. I look up, and there, hanging from a branch, is a black, floppy hat like the one from the stories. My heart jumps. I run hard, catch up to Scruffy by the ditch, and hug him till my breathing slows.

I don’t tell Mom or Dad. Grown-ups go back with you and somehow the thing’s always gone. That’s why kids keep so much to themselves. Instead, I do what Mom says to do: I look up and pray. “God, keep us safe. Protect our house. If there’s a Hat Man out there, keep him away from my family—’specially Sally.”

From now till school starts, I’ll sit up late and listen. If Sally wants my bed, she can have it, no fuss. I even think about asking Daddy if I can keep my hunting knife nearby, but I know knives aren’t toys and I’ll follow whatever rules he sets. I ain’t takin’ no chances.
